task: Write a Python function count_distinct_pairs(nums, target) returning how many distinct unordered value pairs in the list sum to target, each distinct pair counted once no matter how many times its values repeat: [1, 3, 1, 3] with target 4 has exactly one such pair. An empty list returns 0. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The code handles the edge case where an empty list is passed, returning 0 as expected.
trace: I chose to iterate through all possible pairs of numbers in the list and check if their sum equals the target. To avoid counting duplicate pairs, I used a set data structure. If a pair is found where both values are distinct (to account for edge cases such as [1, 2, 2] with target 3), I add it to the set using `frozenset` to ensure the added pair is immutable and thus easily hashable. Finally, the number of unique pairs is returned by the length of the set.
